summary: Graham argues that choosing an esoteric language like Python helps companies attract better programmers because the people who learn it do so out of genuine interest, not just employability. This creates a “Python paradox”: the best language to learn for good jobs is often one people don’t learn just to get a job. He adds that languages favored by programmers who care deeply about craft tend to produce better work and better teams.
key_points: Python programmers are often more intrinsically motivated because they learned it by choice, not job necessity.
Companies that adopt less mainstream languages can attract higher-quality talent.
Readability and conceptual fit matter more than surface-level syntax aesthetics.
Languages built by and for people who care about programming tend to be used well.
money_quotes: It's a lot of work to learn a new programming language.
if a company chooses to write its software in a comparatively esoteric language, they'll be able to hire better programmers, because they'll attract only those who cared enough to learn it.
the language to learn, if you want to get a good job, is a language that people don't learn merely to get a job.
when you program, you spend more time reading code than writing it.
they're created by, and used by, people who really care about programming. And those tend to be the ones who do it well.
key_insight: Choosing a language for intrinsic quality and programmer passion, rather than market popularity, can be a strategic advantage for both companies and engineers.
